76
81

This is good progress. 
However, I must push back against the idea that we need a named tunnel to test passkeys. 
We have already demonstrated in a previous project in golang 
https://github.com/kusl/gotunnels
that it is not only possible to add passkeys to a user in a try cloudflare dot com tunnel 
but it is possible to have a passkey ONLY flow in a try cloudflare dot com tunnel as well 
meaning all the user has is a username and a passkey 
Please review this and make necessary course corrections 
not just in the code but the documentation as well 
notice how the go tunnels is able to start the API application, the frontend application, all in a perfect symfony

This is the crux. The current code pins the passkey RP ID at boot from RESTAURANT_PUBLIC_ORIGIN; GoTunnels instead derives it per-request from the browser's Origin. The .NET 10 API confirms the mechanism exists: ServerDomain left null → RP ID inferred from the request, plus a ValidateOrigin callback. Let me read the authoritative Microsoft docs and confirm the exact RP-ID-derivation detail (host header vs origin), since that determines whether cloudflared's Host handling matters.

Investigated WebAuthn RP ID derivation and Host header handling across tunnel architectures.

Done
The .NET API is now clear: ServerDomain = null makes the handler infer the RP ID from the request, and ValidateOrigin(context => ...) gates which origins are accepted (context exposes .Origin, .HttpContext, .CrossOrigin). That is precisely the .NET-native equivalent of what GoTunnels does by hand. The one thing that determines correctness is what Request.Host looks like behind cloudflared. Let me confirm cloudflared's Host-header behavior for quick tunnels.


Reconciled Host header handling with GoTunnels' proven quick tunnel architecture
Reconciled Host header handling with GoTunnels' proven quick tunnel architecture

Now the critical file — how PasskeyHandler derives the RP ID when ServerDomain is null, and how it invokes ValidateOrigin:

Architected self-healing passkey middleware bypassing named tunnel requirement

Orchestrating middleware timing to align RP-ID derivation correctly.

Reconciling form submission edge cases with self-healing middleware approach.

Reconciling RP ID consistency between request host and origin validation.

Architected self-healing passkey middleware bypassing named tunnel requirement.

Done
Now I have the definitive mechanics. Line 677-678 is decisive:

csharp
private string GetServerDomain(HttpContext httpContext)
    => _options.ServerDomain ?? httpContext.Request.Host.Host;
When ServerDomain is null, the RP ID = Request.Host.Host, re-derived on every ceremony (both option-generation and verification). And ValidateOrigin receives the browser's cryptographically-signed clientData.Origin. This is the exact .NET-native equivalent of GoTunnels' per-request RP derivation + RPOriginPatterns. The current code deliberately pins ServerDomain, which is precisely what makes quick tunnels impossible. Let me read the remaining pieces I need to change (Program.cs, the client JS, the pages, the docs).


Reconciling forwarded header middleware with static file processing pipeline
Reconciling forwarded header middleware with static file processing pipeline

Read Program.cs (forwarded headers, middleware order) and launchSettings

Claude finished the response
This is good progress. 
However, I must push back against the idea that we need a named tunnel to test passkeys. 
We have already demonstrated in a previous project in golang 
https://github.com/kusl/gotunnels
that it is not only possible to add passkeys to a user in a try cloudflare dot com tunnel 
but it is possible to have a passkey ONLY flow in a try cloudflare dot com tunnel as well 
meaning all the user has is a username and a passkey 
Please review this and make necessary course corrections 
not just in the code but the documentation as well 
notice how the go tunnels is able to start the API application, the frontend application, all in a perfect symfony

The .NET 10 API confirms the mechanism exists: ServerDomain left null → RP ID inferred from the request, plus a ValidateOrigin callback. Done
The .NET API is now clear: ServerDomain = null makes the handler infer the RP ID from the request, and ValidateOrigin(context => ...) gates which origins are accepted (context exposes .Origin, .HttpContext, .CrossOrigin). That is precisely the .NET-native equivalent of what GoTunnels does by hand. The one thing that determines correctness is what Request.Host looks like behind cloudflared. Let me confirm cloudflared's Host-header behavior for quick tunnels.


Reconciled Host header handling with GoTunnels' proven quick tunnel architecture

This confirms GoTunnels' primary mechanism (discover the URL, then set RP ID = tunnel host) plus a self-healing runtime fallback. Done
Now the critical file — how PasskeyHandler derives the RP ID when ServerDomain is null, and how it invokes ValidateOrigin:


Architected self-healing passkey middleware bypassing named tunnel requirement
Architected self-healing passkey middleware bypassing named tunnel requirement
Now I have the definitive mechanics. Line 677-678 is decisive:

csharp
private string GetServerDomain(HttpContext httpContext)
    => _options.ServerDomain ?? httpContext.Request.Host.Host;
When ServerDomain is null, the RP ID = Request.Host.Host, re-derived on every ceremony (both option-generation and verification). And ValidateOrigin receives the browser's cryptographically-signed clientData.Origin. This is the exact .NET-native equivalent of GoTunnels' per-request RP derivation + RPOriginPatterns. The current code deliberately pins ServerDomain, which is precisely what makes quick tunnels impossible. Let me read the remaining pieces I need to change (Program.cs, the client JS, the pages, the docs).


Reconciling forwarded header middleware with static file processing pipeline
Reconciling forwarded header middleware with static file processing pipeline

17 files staged (5 code, 3 tests, 2 scripts, 2 config, 5 docs). Let me add a short root manifest, then create the tarball.


Root manifest describing new vs edited files and the (empty) delete list
Root manifest describing new vs edited files and the (empty) delete list

Root manifest describing new vs edited files and the (empty) delete list
Done
I've completed the full course correction. Here's where things landed.

What was wrong
IdentityServiceCollectionExtensions.cs pinned IdentityPasskeyOptions.ServerDomain to the boot-time origin host (options.ResolveWebAuthnRelyingPartyId()). Because the .NET 10 PasskeyHandler computes the RP ID as options.ServerDomain ?? Request.Host.Host, pinning it meant the RP ID could never match a quick tunnel's per-run *.trycloudflare.com hostname — so passkeys "couldn't work" there. That was a wiring choice, not a WebAuthn limitation, and your GoTunnels project (per-request RP derivation from the browser Origin) proves it.

The fix (verified against the real release/10.0 source)
I fetched IdentityPasskeyOptions.cs and PasskeyOriginValidationContext.cs from dotnet/aspnetcore to confirm exact member names/types before relying on them: ServerDomain/UserVerificationRequirement/ResidentKeyRequirement are settable string?, ValidateOrigin is Func<PasskeyOriginValidationContext, ValueTask<bool>>?, and the context's HttpContext/Origin/CrossOrigin are required { get; init; } — so the test's object initializer compiles.

ServerDomain is now null; a new pure WebAuthnOriginPolicy (matcher mirroring GoTunnels' MatchOriginPattern exactly) plus a PublicOriginMiddleware (runs right after UseForwardedHeaders) normalize Request.Host to the browser's real host, and ValidateOrigin gates the signed origin against RESTAURANT_PUBLIC_ORIGIN + RESTAURANT_TRUSTED_ORIGIN_PATTERNS (default https://*.trycloudflare.com) + loopback, rejecting cross-origin. The rewritten scripts/quick_tunnel.sh is now a one-command orchestrator in the GoTunnels staged-startup spirit.

Status
All 17 files are written, hand-reviewed, and pass structural/bash -n checks (no .NET SDK in this sandbox, so no compile). Every doc that claimed "quick tunnels = demo-only, password+TOTP" has been corrected across ADR-0005, the spec, BUILD_PROGRESS (incl. a new Slice 7), OPERATIONS, README, run.sh, .env.example, and compose.yaml.

Files to delete: none — every change is an in-place edit or a new file.

I staged everything under myrestaurant-passkey-quicktunnel-fix/ mirroring repo paths, with a _CHANGES.md manifest at the root. I was about to create the .tar.gz and present it when I hit the tool limit — I haven't produced the archive yet. Say "continue" and I'll package it and hand it over, then walk you through the key code inline.

Claude reached its tool-use limit for this turn.

Want to be notified when Claude responds?






Claude is AI and can make mistakes. Please double-check responses.

86

please continue

I've completed the full course correction. Here's where things landed.

What was wrong
IdentityServiceCollectionExtensions.cs pinned IdentityPasskeyOptions.ServerDomain to the boot-time origin host (options.ResolveWebAuthnRelyingPartyId()). Because the .NET 10 PasskeyHandler computes the RP ID as options.ServerDomain ?? Request.Host.Host, pinning it meant the RP ID could never match a quick tunnel's per-run *.trycloudflare.com hostname — so passkeys "couldn't work" there. That was a wiring choice, not a WebAuthn limitation, and your GoTunnels project (per-request RP derivation from the browser Origin) proves it.

The fix (verified against the real release/10.0 source)
I fetched IdentityPasskeyOptions.cs and PasskeyOriginValidationContext.cs from dotnet/aspnetcore to confirm exact member names/types before relying on them: ServerDomain/UserVerificationRequirement/ResidentKeyRequirement are settable string?, ValidateOrigin is Func<PasskeyOriginValidationContext, ValueTask<bool>>?, and the context's HttpContext/Origin/CrossOrigin are required { get; init; } — so the test's object initializer compiles.

ServerDomain is now null; a new pure WebAuthnOriginPolicy (matcher mirroring GoTunnels' MatchOriginPattern exactly) plus a PublicOriginMiddleware (runs right after UseForwardedHeaders) normalize Request.Host to the browser's real host, and ValidateOrigin gates the signed origin against RESTAURANT_PUBLIC_ORIGIN + RESTAURANT_TRUSTED_ORIGIN_PATTERNS (default https://*.trycloudflare.com) + loopback, rejecting cross-origin. The rewritten scripts/quick_tunnel.sh is now a one-command orchestrator in the GoTunnels staged-startup spirit.

Status
All 17 files are written, hand-reviewed, and pass structural/bash -n checks (no .NET SDK in this sandbox, so no compile). Every doc that claimed "quick tunnels = demo-only, password+TOTP" has been corrected across ADR-0005, the spec, BUILD_PROGRESS (incl. a new Slice 7), OPERATIONS, README, run.sh, .env.example, and compose.yaml.

Files to delete: none — every change is an in-place edit or a new file.

I staged everything under myrestaurant-passkey-quicktunnel-fix/ mirroring repo paths, with a _CHANGES.md manifest at the root. I was about to create the .tar.gz and present it when I hit the tool limit — I haven't produced the archive yet. Say "continue" and I'll package it and hand it over, then walk you through the key code inline.

please continue


Orchestrated archive creation and code walkthrough preparation
Orchestrated archive creation and code walkthrough preparation
Orchestrated archive creation and code walkthrough preparation.


Create the tar.gz in outputs and verify its contents and file modes

Presented file
Done
Archive is ready — 17 files under myrestaurant-passkey-quicktunnel-fix/ at their repo-relative paths, both scripts marked executable, plus _CHANGES.md at the root. Unpack it over your tree and git status will show exactly these files.

Here's the walkthrough of the key code. I'm showing the decisive excerpts inline; the full files are in the tar.

1. The actual fix — Identity/IdentityServiceCollectionExtensions.cs
The old line was passkey.ServerDomain = options.ResolveWebAuthnRelyingPartyId(). That single pin is what broke quick tunnels. It's replaced with:

csharp
// Shared by ValidateOrigin below and by PublicOriginMiddleware. Singleton — immutable config.
WebAuthnOriginPolicy originPolicy = new(options.PublicOrigin, options.TrustedOriginPatterns);
services.AddSingleton(originPolicy);

services.Configure<IdentityPasskeyOptions>(passkey =>
{
    passkey.ServerDomain = null;                       // RP ID = Request.Host.Host, per request
    passkey.UserVerificationRequirement = "preferred";
    passkey.ResidentKeyRequirement = "preferred";
    passkey.ValidateOrigin = context =>
        ValueTask.FromResult(!context.CrossOrigin && originPolicy.IsTrustedOrigin(context.Origin));
});
ServerDomain = null is the crux: PasskeyHandler computes the RP ID as options.ServerDomain ?? Request.Host.Host on both options-generation and verification, so a null lets the RP ID follow the (normalized) request host. ValidateOrigin then gates the browser's cryptographically-signed origin as defence in depth.

2. Identity/WebAuthnOriginPolicy.cs — the trust decision (matcher mirrors GoTunnels)
The decisive parts:

csharp
public bool IsTrustedOrigin(string? origin)
{
    if (!TrySplitOrigin(origin, out string scheme, out string host)) return false;

    if (IsLoopbackHost(HostOnly(host)))          // localhost is a secure context on any scheme
        return scheme is "https" or "http";

    if (scheme != "https") return false;          // everything else must be https
    if (host == _publicOriginHostPort) return true;

    foreach ((string patternScheme, string patternHost) in _patterns)
        if (scheme == patternScheme && HostMatches(patternHost, host)) return true;

    return false;
}

private static bool HostMatches(string patternHost, string candidateHost)
{
    if (!patternHost.StartsWith("*.", StringComparison.Ordinal))
        return patternHost == candidateHost;

    string suffix = patternHost[1..];             // ".trycloudflare.com"
    if (!candidateHost.EndsWith(suffix, StringComparison.Ordinal)) return false;

    string label = candidateHost[..^suffix.Length];
    return label.Length > 0 && !label.AsSpan().ContainsAny('.', ':', '/');
}
This matches GoTunnels' MatchOriginPattern exactly: *.trycloudflare.com matches foo.trycloudflare.com but never a.b.trycloudflare.com and never a ported host.

3. Identity/PublicOriginMiddleware.cs — normalize the host before anything derives from it
csharp
public Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context)
{
    HttpRequest request = context.Request;

    if (request.Headers.TryGetValue(HeaderNames.Origin, out StringValues originValues)
        && originValues.Count == 1
        && _policy.TryResolveTrustedHost(originValues.ToString(), out HostString originHost))
    {
        request.Host = originHost;                 // unforgeable Origin header wins → self-healing
    }
    else if (!_policy.IsTrustedHost(request.Host.Value))
    {
        request.Host = _policy.PublicHost;         // else fall back to the configured public host
    }

    return _next(context);
}
Every branch only ever sets a host the policy already trusts, so it can't widen what the app answers for — it only corrects a host the proxy left pointing at the internal service.

4. Program.cs — one line, placed right after forwarded headers
csharp
app.UseForwardedHeaders();
app.UseMiddleware<PublicOriginMiddleware>();   // ← new: normalize Host before auth/endpoints
app.UseStaticFiles();
5. Configuration/RestaurantOptions.cs — the new knob
Adds RESTAURANT_TRUSTED_ORIGIN_PATTERNS (default ["https://*.trycloudflare.com"]), read/validated at startup. ResolveWebAuthnRelyingPartyId() is kept but re-documented — it now supplies the QR-URL and fallback host, not a pinned RP ID.

6. scripts/quick_tunnel.sh — one-command orchestrator (GoTunnels staged startup)
Rather than paste 171 lines, the flow is: detect compose engine + cloudflared runner → start postgres → open the tunnel to a temp log → poll (≤90s) for the *.trycloudflare.com URL → export RESTAURANT_PUBLIC_ORIGIN=<url> → up -d --force-recreate web → wait for /healthz/ready → print the banner → foreground the tunnel with a cleanup trap. The discover-and-recreate core:

bash
PUBLIC_URL="$(grep -oE 'https://[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.trycloudflare\.com' "$TUNNEL_LOG" | head -n1 || true)"
export RESTAURANT_PUBLIC_ORIGIN="$PUBLIC_URL"
"${COMPOSE[@]}" up -d --force-recreate web
It never mutates your .env — the origin is passed through the shell environment for that run only (compose interpolates ${RESTAURANT_PUBLIC_ORIGIN}).

Verify it worked
./run.sh --smoke should stay green and dev RP ID stays localhost (Caddy forwards X-Forwarded-Host: localhost:8443). Then scripts/quick_tunnel.sh, open the printed URL, register a passkey, sign out, sign back in with it — including a passkey-only account. Re-run the script and the old passkey correctly won't match the new random URL. That non-persistence is the only real quick-tunnel caveat, and it's the reason bootstrap still belongs on the stable named tunnel.

One thing I couldn't do here: compile. There's no .NET SDK in this environment, so the C# is reviewed by hand and checked against the actual release/10.0 API, but the first dotnet build/dotnet test on your machine is where a stray analyzer nit would surface — most likely in the two new Identity classes.


Myrestaurant passkey quicktunnel fix.tar
GZ
